channel.title: Psychology Today – Latest
channel.description: Latest articles from Psychology Today (full content)

item.title: Relaxation or Exercise for Panic Disorder: Which Is Better?
item.description:
Anxiety

Relaxation or Exercise for Panic Disorder: Which Is Better?

A study shows the best method for exposure to feared sensations.

Posted March 3, 2026

Key points
  • Individuals with panic attacks fear the sensations of anxiety within their bodies.
  • Interoceptive exposure involves purposely bringing on body sensations to overcome fears of the symptoms.
  • A recent study found that exercise works like interoceptive exposure and is effective for treating panic.
Source: Ketut Subiyanto/Pexels

We all know that exercise is good for your physical health, and people who struggle with anxiety are often told that they need to relax more. But which is better for reducing panic attack frequency and severity in people diagnosed with panic disorder?

In a recent study in Frontiers in Psychiatry, participants were randomly assigned to one of two conditions: 1) exercise – characterized by walking followed by a brief intense interval sprint (people built up the number of sprints over time); 2) relaxation – which involved progressive muscle relaxation training.

Although both conditions improved, the exercise condition was far superior in terms of reduction of panic frequency and severity, as well as depression symptom reduction at six-month follow-up.

Explaining the Results

While these results might be surprising to some, as a cognitive behavioral therapist, they make complete sense to me. Relaxation training, which used to be part of typical cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT), is now used less frequently. Perhaps the most important treatment component for the treatment of panic disorder is what is called interoceptive exposures.

Let me explain.

People who have panic disorder tend to be afraid of the symptoms of anxiety, such as a racing heart, difficulty breathing, dizziness, and so on. They often catastrophize these symptoms and might think that they are a sign of something being very wrong with their body. As a result, they might avoid doing things like exercising or drinking caffeine that might trigger the attacks.

What interoceptive exposure involves is purposely and systematically eliciting the feared symptoms through a series of exercises, such as running or breathing through a small straw. The idea is to retrain your brain so that you no longer fear the symptoms.

In this study, the exercise served as interoceptive exposure: it gave participants the opportunity to learn that these symptoms are not scary. In addition to this research, other studies have demonstrated that exercise is extremely beneficial for mood symptoms. It is also possible that engaging in a new exercise routine could improve mood and reduce panic symptoms in a symbiotic way.

Implications for Panic Sufferers

This study demonstrates the importance of facing physiological symptoms head-on and that exercise might be an accessible entry point.

It’s important to always check with your doctor before starting a new exercise routine. It’s also important to let them know that you have panic attacks and if you are afraid exercise because of them.

How to Start an Exercise Routine

It can be very difficult to start an exercise routine, especially if you are not currently doing any exercise. Here are some steps you could follow:

  1. Identify your why. When starting a new habit, it’s important to identify why you are doing it and how it fits into your values. It’s not sufficient to just say, I should really start exercising. Instead, how about:
    • I’m going to start exercising because it will help my panic attacks and my mood.
    • I want to start exercising because it will be good for my health.
  2. Decatastrophize in advance. If you tend to have catastrophic thoughts about panic-like symptoms, before you do an exercise session, remind yourself that it is normal and expected that your body physiology will change. When you notice symptoms while you are exercising, you can tell yourself that this is what your body should be doing right now.

  3. Be realistic. If you are not exercising at all, it’s important to be realistic about the frequency and intensity you can maintain. It might not be a realistic goal to go from zero days to six days of intense exercise. Instead, think about what you can realistically set for yourself.

    Can you go for a 30-minute walk two or three times a week? If so, maybe you can do what they did in the study: start with 15 minutes of moderate walking, followed by one 30-second burst of intense running, then 15 minutes of moderate walking. Then slowly gradually increase the number of speed intervals, after you build up some endurance and confidence.

    Or, if you live in a colder climate and going outside for a winter walk is not realistic, is there anything you can do consistently indoors?

  4. Be kind to yourself. If you can’t meet your goal, rather than berating yourself, ask yourself what you could do differently next time
  5. Celebrate the wins. When you can engage in an exercise session, give yourself positive feedback. That will set you up to have the next exercise session.
  • I’m going to start exercising because it will help my panic attacks and my mood.
  • I want to start exercising because it will be good for my health.

The Good News About Panic Disorder

Panic disorder, while it can be extremely debilitating and distressing, is treatable. It can be important to find a therapist who specializes in panic and anxiety, particularly one who will focus on your fear of bodily symptoms. The benefits of this type of therapy often persist long after the treatment has ended.

Read the original article on Psychology Today →
item.dc:creator: Bonnie Zucker Ph.D.

item.title: World Hearing Day Normalizes Me
item.description:
Authenticity

World Hearing Day Normalizes Me

Personal Perspective: For years, I battled shame over my hearing problem.

Posted March 3, 2026

Everyone wears hearing devices.
Source: Gn Hearing Global / Used with Permission

I’ve written before about how, after the birth of my son, I became critically ill, and was in a medical coma in the hospital for two months, and sick for a year. No one expected me to live, but thanks to highly wonky medicines, I did.

But there was a cost to taking those meds. I lost my hair. (No big deal. it grew back better and curlier than ever.) And, I lost much of my hearing. That was a big deal. One that tormented me for years. I didn’t want to get hearing devices because, to me, there was a horrible stigma. People who wore hearing aids were doddering. They didn’t listen, they said, "what, what," over and over. Worse, the hearing aid would make this squealing sound. I worried that it was the beginning of the end of me.

I was so wrong.

World Hearing Day is today, March 3, led globally by the World Health Organization; I am so jazzed to have been appointed the American Ambassador by GN, a global hearing company based in Denmark, which has commissioned research on destigmatizing the use of hearing instruments. To coincide with this day, GN Global is sharing free images (see Unsplash for GN Hearing Global) featuring real people living with hearing loss—a tattooed musician, a young punk woman, a financial wizard in a three-piece suit and spit-shined wingtips. Every walk of life, every kind of person is represented here, all meant to give people who wear hearing devices some normalcy.

Everyone knows the stigma about hearing issues. There are always jokes about a hearing person saying, “Want some pie?” And what the hearing-impaired person hears: “There’s a fly.” Recent movies, like "The Sound of Metal," about a musician who loses his hearing, were upsetting because the hard-of-hearing character lost everything: His music, his girlfriend, and he ended up being an outlier, living an uncomfortable, solitary life. Other movies like "A Quiet Place" use a cochlear implant as a plot device. But what I think people need, what I need, are movies that don’t focus on the trials or empowerment, but on the everyday life of a person who happens to wear a hearing device.

Got flaming hair? Who cares? Got a hearing aid? Good for you!
Source: GN Global Hearing

Recently, my husband and I saw a 1940s film noir about a detective who was great at his job. I noticed he had a wire coming out of his ear, and when he pulled out what looked like a tiny phone, I knew then it was a 1940s hearing aid! No one mentioned his hearing for the entire movie. Instead, it was normalized, no more important than his fedora hat! We need more everyday representations of people living with hearing loss in the media. Let’s hear it (whoops, forgive the pun) for advertisements and movies where a hearing issue is as much a part of a character as the color of their hair.

I went from deep shame (I told no one I had hearing aids for a long time, and I spent a lot of time worrying that people would find out) to realizing the way to get rid of shame is to expose it. Every time I write an essay about my hearing, I feel stronger. Even better, people reach out to me with questions, some of them still shy with shame, others want the name of my audiologist.

My mom used to tell me how embarrassed she was about wearing glasses, how she hid them, because she didn’t want people calling her Four Eyes or dubbing her Mrs. Magoo, making fun of her. Now there’s a Warby Parker on every street corner just about, and people have four or even more pairs of glasses because they’re fun. After all, it’s normal.

Tiny, beautiful, empowering.
Source: GN Global Hearing

Read the original article on Psychology Today →
item.dc:creator: Caroline Leavitt

item.title: Neuroplasticity Across the Lifespan
item.description:
Neuroscience

Neuroplasticity Across the Lifespan

How the brain is adaptable.

Posted March 3, 2026

Key points
  • Brain plasticity is real but constrained by biology and timing.
  • Early experience shapes the brain powerfully, for good or harm.
  • Recovery and learning depend on context, not plasticity alone.

For much of modern history, the brain was seen as largely fixed by the end of childhood. Intelligence, personality, and ability were believed to follow a mostly predetermined biological path. According to this view, you were born with a certain kind of brain, and its basic structure would not change in any meaningful way. Over the past few decades, neuroscience has steadily revised that assumption. Researchers now speak of brain plasticity, the capacity of the brain to alter its structure and function in response to experience.

This has transformed our view on the ways in which we perceive development, learning, injury and aging. Due to our increased understanding of how experience shapes our development, the importance of past experiences will have greater significance than what we have previously acknowledged. This will enable individuals to recover from injuries, and to extend or change the rate of decline in their late years. However, much of the information regarding plasticity is presented as an unlimited capacity. However, studies indicate that plasticity is limited by biological boundaries.

What Plasticity Involves

Brain plasticity refers to physical changes in neural tissue. Synapses can strengthen or weaken depending on how frequently they are used. Neural circuits can reorganize, expanding or contracting their functional territory. In certain regions, especially the hippocampus, new neurons can be generated and incorporated into existing networks, a process associated with learning and memory (May, 2011). These changes occur on different timescales. Some are brief and reversible, while others are more enduring.

Musicians show altered sensory maps in areas representing the fingers they use most intensely. Individuals who have experienced a stroke sometimes relearn movements once thought permanently lost, reflecting reorganization in surviving brain tissue. Children raised in environments lacking adequate sensory or social input show measurable structural and functional differences compared with those raised in enriched settings (Kolb & Whishaw, 1998). Findings such as these have helped dismantle the long-standing belief that adult brains are incapable of meaningful change.

At the same time, defining plasticity is not straightforward. Some researchers use the term broadly to include all neural change, whether temporary or lasting, beneficial or maladaptive. Others argue that this risks weakening its scientific meaning. Is plasticity only about improvement, or does it also include harmful adaptations to stress and trauma? These debates matter, because how plasticity is defined influences how it is studied and how its implications are interpreted outside the laboratory.

Limits and Sensitive Periods

Brain plasticity does not mean the brain can change or adapt to everything throughout one's life. Brain development is a genetically based program or set of instructions that has sensitive periods within which the individual can successfully develop those instructions. One example of this is young children’s ability to make distinctions between subtle differences of phonetics, such as "t" as in tower versus "r" as in river, which adults often cannot distinguish. Even with continued plasticity of the adult brain, the structures and pathways formed through prior experiences will inhibit the ability to change. Therefore, while learning creates some level of structure, it also creates a framework by which certain things can be learned.

Neuroimaging research supports the idea that experience can reshape the brain. Studies have shown increases in gray matter density following intensive training, suggesting structural adaptation to new demands (Draganski et al., 2004). However, interpreting these changes is not always simple. It can be difficult to determine whether brain differences are caused by training or whether individuals with certain preexisting brain features are more likely to pursue and excel at particular skills. Longitudinal studies help, but they are challenging to conduct.

Early Experience and Development

Plasticity reaches its fullest scope in childhood. Neural connections are developing rapidly during the first few years of life. The arrangement of the circuits in the brain has been highly influenced by what a child experiences during those first few years of life. A rich early environment, one that provides language, emotional security and cognitive stimulation, aids in developing the neural networks a child needs for future competence, whereas chronic stress, neglect and deprivation will change normal development in ways that will last well into adulthood (Kolb & Whishaw, 1998). Adverse experiences do not entirely dictate someone's future, but they help determine how the brain's plasticity will create adaptations to the environment.

The plasticity that occurs within the child is both a source of opportunity and risk. The mechanisms that enable us to learn quickly can also put our developing brains at risk from poorer conditions.

Aging and Adaptation

As we age, many of us go through periods of slower cognitive processing and changes to our memory. However, we can still develop and change as we age. Studies show that physical activity, learning new skills, and participating in social environments positively impact the structural and/or functional organization of the brain (May, 2011). Although these changes may be less dramatic than those associated with younger individuals, they provide older adults with a sense of resilience and aid in maintaining independence.

Another example of plasticity is recovery from an acquired brain injury. After suffering a brain injury, the remaining neural networks within the brain must adapt to the damaged portions of the brain; therefore, rehabilitation programs are designed to support brain connections through the practice of specific activities. Recovery from an injury varies for each individual, influenced by multiple factors including the severity and area of the injury, onset and timing of therapy, and client/ caregiver health and social supports (Merzenich, 2014). Thus, plasticity provides the opportunity for improvement but does not guarantee complete recovery.

A Measured View of Possibility

The growing public discourse around brain plasticity is extremely positive, leading some people to think they can consciously create new brain circuitry and improve performance. Evidence for plasticity supports that the brain might be capable of changing functionally. However, it also indicates that change within the brain is limited by biological, temporal, and experience-dependent constraints. It means not all structural changes within the brain result in positive changes in ability; a change may represent compensatory mechanisms or responses to a negative environment rather than improvements. Plasticity cannot be understood without accepting that there are differences between what is possible and what is certain to occur. The brain does not continually recreate itself indefinitely or on its own but rather as a living organ with biological systems and windows of opportunity. There is substantial evidence from longitudinal studies of plasticity across development, education/training, aging, and injury (i.e., Kolb & Whishaw, 1998; Draganski et al., 2004; May, 2011; Merzenich, 2014) documenting brain structural and functional changes, and similarly there is evidence to define the limits of plasticity.

Plasticity is not a miracle. Nor is plasticity myth, rather it is one of the fundamental biological properties that underlie our ability to adapt through the human lifespan. Understanding the possibilities and limitations of brain plasticity can help provide realistic expectations of learning and recovery, and furthermore, clarify our potential as human beings.

Read the original article on Psychology Today →
item.dc:creator: Common Sense Science

item.title: No One Is Coming to Help—Except Your Neighbors
item.description:
Resilience

No One Is Coming to Help—Except Your Neighbors

True community resilience today requires organizing local resilience networks.

Posted March 3, 2026

Key points
  • Americans are experiencing numerous interacting perils today, including climate change and rising inequality.
  • As problems arise, neighbors and strangers have been supporting each other and setting up mutual aid networks.
  • Forming community-led resilience networks nationwide could help us address the overlapping crises we face.

Recently, I met with a group near Asheville, North Carolina, and asked them how residents dealt with the prolific damage caused by Hurricane Helene that hammered their area in 2024. They all said neighbors—and strangers—helped one another.

Neighbors helping people they know and don't know is always key to surviving calamities and activating positive solutions. With compounding adversities now happening everywhere, building robust social support networks and mutual aid groups in every neighborhood and community should be a top priority. This requires forming local resilience networks nationwide.

The Biggest Challenges Facing Americans Today

Americans today are being impacted by multiple accumulating perils. There are too many to list here, so I am going to focus on just three of the most pressing.

1. Violence by the Federal Government

One hazard that many people are experiencing, at least in certain communities, is aggression and violence by federal Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agents, which has traumatized immigrants and countless others where it occurs. Yet when ICE appears, what has often kept people safe is neighbors helping neighbors (and strangers). For instance, in many communities, residents are distributing flyers and running workshops on how residents can prepare for and respond to ICE raids, or using community hotlines and whistles to alert people when agents are spotted. These and other actions show that crises can bring residents together to support others they don't know and press for beneficial change.

2. Record Economic Inequality and Related Job and Income Struggles

Another very serious hardship millions of Americans are experiencing is skyrocketing economic inequality. In the U.S., inequality now resembles the pre-Great Depression conditions of a century ago and is by far the largest of all high-income G7 nations. The widespread distress generated by inequality has significantly reduced social cohesion and trust, created poorer health outcomes, produced major job and income struggles, and increased poverty.

But again, in many communities, it is neighbors who take the lead in addressing financial hardships. Examples include neighbors reducing food insecurity by establishing public refrigerators and pantry boxes that allow people to obtain free food. Neighbors also often play important roles in creating better jobs and higher incomes by, for example, establishing local businesses such as shared commercial kitchens to launch companies in the food industry.

3. The Damage and Losses Caused by the Climate-Ecosystem-Biodiversity (C-E-B) Crisis

Unless humanity quickly slashes emissions, within five years temperatures in some parts of the world are likely to rise by close to 2 °C (3.6 °F), at least temporarily. By 2045, temperatures are likely to rise by 2 °C permanently everywhere. More frequent, prolonged, surprising, and destructive weather disasters are now certain. But just as neighbors are stepping forward to help others deal with ICE agents and navigate economic struggles, it is always locals who spontaneously assist others during disasters.

Some residents put their lives on-the-line to save people they don't even know. Neighbors also join together to voluntarily provide practical assistance, as well as food, water, shelter, medical care, emotional support, and other vital needs and services to others during and after a disaster. This organic process is called the "community cohesion" or "honeymoon" phase of a disaster. Following calamities, residents freely come together to provide each other with mutual aid. The process builds connections and trust.

However, the community cohesion phase usually lasts just a few weeks or months, after which the "disillusionment phase" of a disaster kicks in, in which people struggle to mourn what was lost and put their lives back together. For many, the disillusionment phase can last for months or years, and the distress people often feel can generate serious social, psychological, and emotional issues.

Effective Community Resilience Requires Maintaining "Community Cohesion" Into Perpetuity

The many current and future adversities Americans will experience underscore the urgency of forming the social infrastructure needed to maintain the "community cohesion" phase of disasters into perpetuity and prevent the "disillusionment" phase from occurring.

Organizing local resilience networks in neighborhoods and communities everywhere is the most effective way to achieve this.

Effective community resilience is a collective activity in which people join with others to respond to a shared crisis. A number of factors have been identified that produce these outcomes. They include the strength and extent of social connections, psychological cohesiveness, and trust among local residents, their level of attachment to place, vision of success, and belief in community leaders' capacity to effectively pursue the vision.

These factors combine to determine the level of collective efficacy that exists in a community. This is the belief among residents in their capacity to manage and respond constructively to crises and achieve their collective vision and goals.

Building collective efficacy requires that the top priority is to proactively strengthen the human social, psychological, emotional, and behavioral dimensions of resilience. This requires engaging residents in strengthening "protective factors," with the most important being forming robust networked social support systems and mutual aid networks, to buffer them from and help them push back against severe stresses and traumas.

To prevent and heal mental health, psychosocial, and other issues generated by accelerating adversities, forming resilience networks in every neighborhood and community should now be a top national priority.

Read the original article on Psychology Today →
item.dc:creator: Bob Doppelt

item.title: Fluent at Home, Silent at Work: Growing Up Bilingual
item.description:
Education

Fluent at Home, Silent at Work: Growing Up Bilingual

Why so many Hispanic professionals doubt their Spanish.

Posted March 3, 2026

Key points
  • Many heritage speakers do not receive the type of language instruction they need.
  • Shame-based language correction causes people to stop using it.
  • The right instruction changes more than language; it changes how people see themselves.
Source: Don Cangrejo / Unsplash

Many Hispanic professionals in the U.S. carry a quiet, persistent belief that their Spanish isn't good enough. They speak it at home, with family, in their communities. They move between English and Spanish with ease in daily life. But ask them to write a formal email in Spanish, present at a conference, or draft a professional document, and something shifts, which makes them hesitate and retreat toward English. An internal voice says: not here, not this.

That voice has a history. And understanding where it comes from is the first step toward recognizing it for what it actually is: the psychological residue of a system that was never built for them.

A Name for an Experience

Linguists use the term heritage speaker to describe someone who grew up in a household where a language other than the societal dominant was spoken — in the U.S., most commonly Spanish. Heritage speakers acquired their language naturally, at home, through daily life. What they typically did not receive is formal education in that language.

This distinction matters enormously. Formal schooling doesn't just teach grammar; it builds vocabulary across professional and academic domains, develops the ability to shift between registers (e.g., the way language changes between a family dinner and a job interview) and gives speakers the tools to use their language in public, institutional contexts. Heritage speakers learned to speak. They were rarely taught to write, to argue formally, or to inhabit the professional range of their language.

When they encounter that gap later in life, the tendency is to experience it as personal failure. But the gap was created by educational systems that didn't offer them the right instruction at the right time.

What Happens When the Class Is Wrong

Many heritage speakers do eventually end up in a Spanish class, often a beginner course designed for students who have never spoken the language. The experience is almost universally discouraging. The pacing is slow, the content obvious, and the grammatical exercises (conjugating verbs in a dozen tenses, memorizing vocabulary they already know) feel pointless. Many disengage quickly. Some conclude that they are simply "bad" at Spanish.

Nevertheless, research shows that, in many respects, heritage speakers are ahead. Because they already have a linguistic foundation, they acquire new registers and formal skills significantly faster than learners starting from zero. The problem is that beginner courses were not designed for people with their linguistic history, and when those courses fail to challenge or engage them, the failure gets internalized as their own.

This produces a sort of psychological injury with lasting effects: an avoidance of Spanish in professional contexts, a reluctance to claim bilingual identity, and chronic self-monitoring whenever formal language is required.

The Double Bind

What compounds this is the social pressure heritage speakers face from both directions. In mainstream professional settings, speaking Spanish can mark someone as less fluent in English, less assimilated, less belonging. In family or community spaces, speaking Spanish with an American accent or gaps in vocabulary invites a different judgment: that their Spanish is broken, incomplete, not real. Too Spanish for some spaces. Not Spanish enough for others.

Semester after semester, I watch the same pattern unfold. Students who speak Spanish confidently with their families arrive at college and go quiet the moment a formal context requires it. They defer to classmates in professional Spanish conversations, reach for English when anything important needs to be said, and hedge when asked whether they're bilingual, as if claiming it fully might invite a test they're not sure they can pass. Over time, that silence stops feeling like a strategy and starts feeling like the truth.

The correction that heritage speakers receive along the way often reinforces this. When the message (implicit or explicit) is that the variety of Spanish shaped by their bilingual lives is wrong or inferior, it doesn't motivate improvement. Shame-based correction undermines both motivation and acquisition. People stop using the language they've been told they're using incorrectly.

What Resolution Looks Like

Effective heritage language education works differently, and the psychological shift it produces is striking. Rather than drilling grammar in isolation, the most successful programs engage heritage speakers with meaningful texts: reading and producing academic articles, professional emails, policy documents, personal narratives. Accuracy develops through use, not as a prerequisite for it. Correction is reframed: the home variety is treated as a legitimate foundation, and developing formal registers is presented as expansion rather than replacement.

The change students describe is in how they understand themselves. There is a particular moment that many heritage language educators recognize: when a student discovers that there is a name for their experience, that their linguistic profile is the predictable result of a specific history rather than evidence of personal inadequacy. That reframe —finding language for something that had only ever felt like failure — is genuinely transformative. Students who had avoided Spanish in professional or academic contexts begin to use it. Those who had spent years doubting themselves start to claim their bilingualism with confidence.

This matters beyond the individual. Hispanic professionals who develop the full range of their bilingual capacity don't simply fill a gap in the labor market, though the need is real and documented across healthcare, law, education, and public service. They also become agents of change inside their institutions, demonstrating through their own practice what bilingualism looks like at the highest levels of professional life.

But that outcome depends on something the field is still working to provide consistently: institutions and workplaces that treat bilingual capacity as the asset it is, programs designed specifically for heritage learners rather than adapted from courses built for someone else, and a cultural shift in how we understand the difference between a language someone was never taught and a language they don't know.

Read the original article on Psychology Today →
item.dc:creator: Cristina Lozano-Argüelles Ph.D.

item.title: Why Peer Justice Is the Secret to a High-Performing Team
item.description:
Teamwork

Why Peer Justice Is the Secret to a High-Performing Team

Beyond the Boss: How to foster peer justice in your organization.

Posted March 3, 2026

Key points
  • Peer justice emphasizes fairness among coworkers, impacting job satisfaction and team dynamics.
  • High peer justice fuels group learning, encouraging employees to embrace challenges and innovate.
  • Lack of peer justice can lead to knowledge hiding, envy, and reduced cooperation among team members.
  • Fostering peer justice requires ethical leadership and a culture of mutual respect and collaboration.

by Giang Hoang, Ph.D. and Tony Nguyen, Ph.D.

In the realm of organizational psychology, the focus has often been on the relationship between employees and their managers. We routinely ask questions like, "Is the boss fair?" and "Does the supervisor listen?" However, as modern workplaces evolve to become flatter and more collaborative, a new form of fairness is emerging as a critical driver of success: peer justice. This concept shifts attention from the authority figure at the top of the organizational chart to the individuals in adjacent cubicles or the teammates on your Zoom call.

What Is Peer Justice?

Peer justice refers to the perceived fairness of the treatment that employees receive from their coworkers, rather than from formal authorities (Estreder et al., 2024). While traditional organizational justice focuses on supervisors and hierarchical relationships, peer justice emphasizes the horizontal connections that shape our daily work experiences.

Peer justice manifests in several key dimensions (Estreder et al., 2024; Walumbwa et al., 2017):

  • Informational Fairness: Are your colleagues sharing the essential information you need to perform your job effectively, or are they keeping you in the dark?
  • Interpersonal Fairness: Are your teammates treating you with courtesy, dignity, and respect?
  • Procedural and Distributive Fairness: Are decisions made by the team—such as how to divide workloads—consistent and unbiased?

Research indicates that peer justice is highly dynamic; it is not a static "vibe" but fluctuates based on daily interactions. In fact, one study found that about 70% of the variance in peer justice perceptions occurs within the same individual from day to day (Estreder et al., 2024). This emphasizes the importance of cultivating a positive workplace environment where fairness is consistently practiced.

Why It Matters: The Power of Peer Fairness

You might assume that as long as the boss is fair, the team will thrive. However, peer justice has a unique impact on performance that even the best manager cannot replicate.

  1. Fueling Group Learning: Peer justice is a primary driver of "group learning behavior." When employees perceive their peers as fair, they are more likely to seek new skills, embrace challenges, and take risks on innovative ideas. This culture of learning not only enhances individual capabilities but also boosts overall team performance.
  2. Boosting Performance and Satisfaction: Higher levels of peer justice correlate directly with increased job satisfaction and in-role performance. When we feel respected by our equals, our productivity naturally improves. Research shows that teams characterized by high peer justice often outperform those that lack it, as members are more engaged and committed to collective goals.
  3. Encouraging Helping Behaviors: Peer justice is one of the strongest predictors of whether employees will go out of their way to assist their coworkers. This creates a virtuous cycle of mutual support, fostering an environment where collaboration thrives, and team members feel empowered to contribute to one another's success.

The Hidden Costs of Unfairness

When peer justice is lacking, the consequences can be detrimental to an organization (Hoang et al., 2025):

  • Knowledge Hiding. In environments where fairness is low—often exacerbated by competitive leadership styles—employees may intentionally withhold information from peers. This "knowledge hiding" stifles creativity and hinders organizational progress, leading to missed opportunities and reduced innovation.
  • Employee Envy. A lack of fairness among peers can trigger intense feelings of envy, turning a competitive atmosphere into a breeding ground for counterproductive behaviors. This emotional turmoil can erode trust and cooperation, further damaging team dynamics.
  • Reduced Cooperation. Without the glue of peer justice, social exchanges between coworkers deteriorate. Instead of functioning as a cohesive unit, the team becomes a collection of individuals focused solely on their self-interests, undermining collective goals and diminishing overall effectiveness.

How to Foster Peer Justice in Your Organization

Creating a culture of peer fairness requires more than just policies; it necessitates a fundamental shift in leadership and team dynamics (Walumbwa et al., 2017).

  • Integrate Ethical Leadership. Leaders who prioritize results and competition can inadvertently undermine peer justice. By modeling "normatively appropriate conduct"—such as honesty, transparency, and shared decision-making—leaders set a standard for how coworkers should treat one another. Ethical leadership fosters an environment where fairness is valued and practiced.
  • Promote Group Ethical Conduct. Encourage teams to develop their own standards for ethical behavior, which naturally fosters peer justice. Facilitate discussions that define what "fairness" looks like in their specific context, allowing team members to take ownership of their workplace culture.
  • Leverage Justice as a Buffer. Peer justice can protect employees from the negative effects of high-pressure leadership. By cultivating a supportive atmosphere where colleagues feel treated justly, organizations can maintain high performance without the risk of burnout or knowledge hiding.
  • Training and Culture. Design training programs that focus not only on individual skills but also on cultivating a collaborative workplace culture. Encourage team-building activities that emphasize the importance of fairness and mutual respect, reinforcing the value of peer justice.

Conclusion

The next time you evaluate your team’s health, don’t just look up at management; look across the table. The fairness that exists between peers may be the most crucial factor in your organization's success. By prioritizing peer justice, organizations can unlock the full potential of their teams, fostering collaboration, innovation, and sustained high performance. Embracing this concept is not just beneficial; it is essential for thriving in today’s dynamic work environment. In a world where teamwork and collaboration are paramount, peer justice stands as a cornerstone of organizational effectiveness and employee well-being.

Read the original article on Psychology Today →
item.dc:creator: Giang Hoang Ph.D.
